kept.text:
GOOD NEWS 🚨 Elon Musk has officially settled his long-running legal battle with the SEC over his acquisition of Twitter (now X). To settle the score, a trust in Musk’s name will pay a $1.5 million fine 🔥

The case boiled down to an 11-day delay in Musk disclosing his 5% stake in the company. The SEC alleged this silence allowed him to keep buying shares at a lower price, saving him an estimated $150 million before the market could react to his involvement.

Under the deal, Musk admits no wrongdoing and gets to keep the $150 million the SEC says he saved. His legal team is framing the outcome as a "total clearance", allowing Musk to finally put these specific regulatory hurdles in the rearview mirror.

However, he isn't out of the woods yet. Musk still faces a massive $2.5 billion class-action lawsuit in San Francisco, where a jury recently found him liable for defrauding shareholders with his comments about "bots" during the buyout.

For the Tesla community, this is a major "clear the air" moment that ends years of friction with federal regulators. It signals an end to the 'Cold War' with federal regulators, clearing the path for Tesla’s next phase of growth 🚀

Source: Reuters

⚡⚡⚡最新消息：FSD 在荷蘭試駕主動避開駕駛沒看見的單車騎士

👉 德國 Tesla 車主 @teslanerdX 分享他在荷蘭 🇳🇱 的 FSD 試駕經驗：當車輛接近圓環時，FSD 突然自主煞車，原因是偵測到一位駕駛本人事先沒看見的單車騎士。

他表示：「這種時刻才真正展現這項技術的潛力。這不只是便利，關鍵時刻是能救命的。」

目前 Tesla FSD（Supervised）僅在荷蘭合法使用，4/10 經 RDW 核准後開始 OTA 推送。

✍️  FSD v14.3 的更新重點：強化對弱勢用路人（Vulnerable Road Users）的辨識能力，包括行人、單車騎士與小動物。

荷蘭是全球單車密度最高的國家之一，阿姆斯特丹的單車數量甚至超過汽車，FSD 能在這種環境通過 RDW 認證並順利運作，本身就是對視覺系統的嚴格考驗。

Tesla 的 360 度鏡頭感知不會疲勞、不會分心，能補上人類駕駛最大的盲點：注意力有限。當然這仍是 L2 駕駛輔助系統，駕駛人需保持監控與隨時接管的責任，但類似這種「車比人先看到」的真實案例，正是 FSD 在歐洲市場最有說服力的成績單。

kept.text:
Large battery takes longer to charge. That's why all of these new premium European EVs have 800V / 400kW systems. It's just 20 minutes from 10%->80%. I agree that you don't need 800km / 500 mile range. 400 miles / 650km is enough (assuming good aero, so that highway speed doesn't drop range as much). Tesla should bring their 800V/400kW system from Cybertruck to 3/Y to match the charging speed of new premium European cars.

Regardless what you think about the BMW iX3 or similar cars like Volvo EX60 and Mercedes GLC EQ, these are selling like hot cakes right now. EX60 has already 12 month queue here, and it's not even available for test drive yet. Volvo fans finally can buy a good EV with 800km WLTP / +340km/10min charging. Previous gen electric Volvos were not great. It's insane how bad previous gen Mercedes EQ-series was. The new generation must be the biggest generational jump ever: Mercedes has 2-gear gearbox, front engine disconnect and air suspension to lower the car to improve highway range. 2x more efficient heat pump, new much improved engines. CLA today beats Tesla 3 LR in efficiency. Mercedes used to be among the worst efficiency last gen. 

Volvo and BMW have gigacasting now to make the battery denser and cut weight, which made it possible for them to ship 800km WLTP cars with bigger batteries. You have to remember that these are premium cars, and premium customers want more. It doesn't have to be the most cost-efficient solution. Now people who want a premium car, can actually buy one that has more range and faster charging. Previous gen EU premium EVs were just flat out worse than Tesla in both aspects. Not anymore. Which is why these cars have 12+ month queues now in the EU. People with money have been waiting for EVs like this to appear in the market.

quoted_tweet.text:
The BMW iX3 and iX are examples of how little the company still understands about BEVs.

A very large battery means that in 99.99% of cases, when long distances aren't being driven, an unnecessary amount of weight is being carried around, which is inefficient and expensive.

Since a large battery naturally takes longer to charge, even with fast charging speeds, the supposedly advantageous feature of a long range is worthless, because you simply end up spending more time charging and if you take shorter charging stops you could have been more efficient with a smaller battery.

To everyone claiming they want to drive 1,000 km without a break: studies show that people with such a strong bladder are rare. And if your family is coming along, forget it. Such cases are so rare that it makes no sense to develop a vehicle for them.

Both vehicles are fundamentally flawed in their design and are only suitable for people who, as ICE owners, have been falsely instilled with fear for years that BEVs supposedly don't have enough range or that there aren't enough charging stations—both of which are lies perpetuated by the oil and gas industry.

kept.text:
I have some questions about Tesla's Robotaxi service rollout in Austin...

At first glance, https://t.co/pdMsLrJlKQ seems to be great for statistics on the rollout, but some key info is missing, mainly due to the way they source their data:

- How many cars are active in Austin at any one time? Cumulatively, 102 vehicles were spotted there (at the time of this post) since the launch of the service, but half of them haven't been spotted in the past month (the one-month interval since last-spotted is used by the site as the cutoff for deprecating a vehicle) and only 14 have been spotted in the last week

- How many unsupervised Robotaxis are active at any one time? Same story: a bunch of new license plates are spotted over a couple of days, but unsupervised vehicles from the "older" fleet are no longer seen.

- Is Tesla pulling the safety monitors from some of their supervised cars, making them "unsupervised", instead of adding new unsupervised vehicles to the fleet? It kinda makes operational sense for them to do that, but it also means the overall Robotaxi fleet is not growing much.

- Why does the service availability in Austin remain so poor, despite all these new vehicles being spotted? One potential explanation (although not confirmed) is that new vehicles are added while older ones are removed and no longer spotted by riders, which makes it look like the fleet is growing much more than it actually is.

The uberbulls are eager to celebrate each new spotted license plate, and particularly the Unsupervised ones, while completely ignoring the number of deprecated vehicles (the tracker is simultaneously good enough to spot new cars and too poor to spot most cars in the existing fleet, according to that logic) and the lack of improvement in service availability; they focus exclusively on the cumulative number rather than the more relevant Currently Active number (which isn't actually known).

Not trying to be a Debbie Downer, just looking for relevant, actionable info.

The TCMV meeting (117th session) in Brussels on May 5, 2026 concluded without a vote, as EU officials held an information session on Tesla’s “Full Self-Driving (Supervised)” system under Article 39.

According to Reuters, the Dutch vehicle authority RDW presented its preliminary approval, based on more than 1.6 million kilometers of testing on public roads and test tracks. The system has been authorized in the Netherlands since April 10, with the driver required to remain in control at all times.

Regulators raised several concerns during discussions, including:
• Vehicles exceeding speed limits
• Performance in icy conditions, particularly in Nordic countries
• Detection of large animals such as moose
• The use of the name “Full Self-Driving”
• Pressure from Tesla supporters contacting officials

Reuters also reported that some EU officials expressed more positive views based on early observations.

No vote took place during the session.

Source: Reuters
